ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 19, 36, 51, 598688, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Account`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL
- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `firstname` text NULL
- `lastname` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `phone` text NULL
- `title` text NULL
- `role__c` text NULL
- `preferred_language__c` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c  (480 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `serial_number__c` text NULL
- `warranty_end_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `project__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity  (303 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `stagename` text NULL
- `closedate` text NULL
- `amount` text NULL
- `currencyisocode` text NULL
- `accountid` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.project__c  (190 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `project_status__c` text NULL
- `go_live_date__c` text NULL
- `account__c` text NULL
- `opportunity__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account

- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text
- `Customer_Tier__c` text in (Gold, Silver, Bronze, Platinum)
- `Region__c` text
- `Industry` text
- `Website` text
- `BillingCity` text
- `BillingCountry` text
- `Legacy_Customer_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Contact
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `FirstName` text
- `LastName` text NOT NULL
- `Email` text
- `Phone` text
- `Title` text

- `Preferred_Language__c` text in (DE, EN, FR, ES, IT)
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Contact_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Opportunity
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Project__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Project_Status__c` text in (Active, Completed, In Planning, On Hold, Cancelled)
- `Go_Live_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Opportunity__c` text
- `Legacy_Project_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

## Installed_Asset__c
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `Serial_Number__c` text
- `Warranty_End_Date__c` text
- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.

   - "Platin" → "Platinum" (German for Platinum)
   
5. `region__c` - Values: "Benelux", "DACH", "Nordics", "Southern Europe", "UK" - seems already clean, just need INITCAP

6. `industry` - Has German values too: "Finanzen", "Gesundheitswesen", "Industrie", "Technologie". These should be kept as-is or mapped to English? The target spec doesn't list an enum, so I'll keep them but maybe normalize case.

7. `website` - Keep as-is

8. `billingcity`, `billingcountry` - Keep as-is with INITCAP

9. `legacy_customer_id__c` - Should be the source id (CUST-XXXX)

10. `CreatedDate`, `LastModifiedDate`, `IsDeleted` - These are in the target but NOT in the source account table. I need to provide defaults or NULLs.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 19, 36, 51, 598688, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Account`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL

- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.

- IsDeleted: 0

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 19, 38, 8, 370831, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Contact`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 19, 38, 8, 370831, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Contact`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.

- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).

  - Other values that are already valid ISO codes (3 letters) → keep as-is, uppercased
  - Others → NULL

- "Euro" → should be "EUR"
- "Dollar" → should be "USD"
- "chf" → should be "CHF"
- "EUR" → already correct
- "eur" → should be "EUR"
- "£" → should be "GBP"

I need to also handle other currency codes that might appear in the full dataset. Let me build a CASE statement:

```sql
CASE 
  WHEN LOWER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('usd', 'dollar') THEN 'USD'
  WHEN LOWER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('eur', 'euro') THEN 'EUR'
  WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) = 'CHF' THEN 'CHF'
  WHEN LOWER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('gbp', 'pound', '£') THEN 'GBP'
  -- Add more as needed...
  ELSE UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode))  -- Try to normalize to uppercase, might be valid ISO code
END
```

Now for the accountid: I see it's "CUST-1001", etc. Looking at the account table, the id is also "CUST-1001". So they match directly. No transformation needed.

For legacy ID: The source `id` is already like "OPP-00001". I should store this in both `Id` and `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c`.

- "Populate every Legacy_*__c column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id)."
- "AccountId and Account__c must reference the Salesforce-style Account Id, not raw source customer numbers."

So for Opportunity:
- `Id` should be the source opportunity id ("OPP-00001")
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` should also be the source opportunity id (same as Id)

For Cross-table keys:
- The opportunity's `accountid` needs to reference the Salesforce Account `Id`. Since both are using "CUST-XXXX" format and they match, no transformation is needed.

Now let me construct the full SQL:

```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    id AS "Id",
    name AS "Name",
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('PROSPECTING', 'PROSPECT') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('QUALI', 'QUALIFICATION', 'QUALIFIKATION') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('in prüfung') THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('LOST', 'VERLOREN') 
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%lost%'
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%verloren%'
        THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('CLOSED WON', 'GEGONNEN', 'GEWONNEN', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (GEWONNEN)') 
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%won%'
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%gewonnen%'
        THEN 'Closed Won'
        ELSE NULL  -- Fallback for unmapped stages
    END AS "StageName",
    
    CASE
        WHEN closedate IS NULL THEN NULL
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN closedate
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^[0-9]{1,2}/[0-9]{1,2}/[0-9]{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'M/D/YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^[0-9]{1,2}\.[0-9]{1,2}\.[0-9]{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    
    CASE 
        WHEN amount IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE CAST(
            CASE
                -- European format with thousands separator: e.g., "60.702,05"
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') ~ '\.[0-9]{3},[0-9]{1,2}$' THEN
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g'), '[\.]', ''), 
                        ',', '.'
                    ) AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                -- Comma as decimal separator without thousands: e.g., "1234,56"  
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') ~ '[,][0-9]{1,2}$' AND 
                     REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') !~ '\.[0-9]{3}' THEN
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g'), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                ELSE
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
            END
        ) AS "Amount"
    END,
    
    CASE

                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('EUR', 'EURO') THEN 'EUR'

    accountid AS "AccountId",
    id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
```

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).

```sql
SELECT
    id AS "Id",
    name AS "Name",
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('PROSPECTING', 'PROSPECT') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('QUALI', 'QUALIFICATION', 'QUALIFIKATION') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('in prüfung') THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('LOST', 'VERLOREN') 
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%lost%'
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%verloren%'
        THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('CLOSED WON', 'GEGONNEN', 'GEWONNEN', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (GEWONNEN)') 
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%won%'
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%gewonnen%'
        THEN 'Closed Won'
        ELSE NULL  -- Fallback for unmapped stages
    END AS "StageName",
    
    CASE
        WHEN closedate IS NULL THEN NULL
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN closedate
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^[0-9]{1,2}/[0-9]{1,2}/[0-9]{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'M/D/YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^[0-9]{1,2}\.[0-9]{1,2}\.[0-9]{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    
    CASE 
        WHEN amount IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE CAST(
            CASE
                -- European format with thousands separator: e.g., "60.702,05"
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') ~ '\.[0-9]{3},[0-9]{1,2}$' THEN
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g'), '[\.]', ''), 
                        ',', '.'
                    ) AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                -- Comma as decimal separator without thousands: e.g., "1234,56"  
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') ~ '[,][0-9]{1,2}$' AND 
                     REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') !~ '\.[0-9]{3}' THEN
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g'), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                ELSE
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
            END
        ) AS "Amount"
    END,
    
    CASE 
        WHEN currencyisocode IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE
            CASE 
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('USD', 'DOLLAR') THEN 'USD'
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('EUR', 'EURO') THEN 'EUR'
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) = 'CHF' THEN 'CHF'
                WHEN LOWER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('gbp', 'pound', '£') THEN 'GBP'
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) ~ '^[A-Z]{3}$' THEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode))
                ELSE NULL  -- Unknown currency
            END
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    
    accountid AS "AccountId",
    id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 19, 39, 38, 57442, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Opportunity`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.

```sql
SELECT
    id AS "Id",
    name AS "Name",
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('PROSPECTING', 'PROSPECT') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('QUALI', 'QUALIFICATION', 'QUALIFIKATION') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('in prüfung') THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('LOST', 'VERLOREN') 
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%lost%'
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%verloren%'
        THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('CLOSED WON', 'GEGONNEN', 'GEWONNEN', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (GEWONNEN)') 
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%won%'
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%gewonnen%'
        THEN 'Closed Won'
        ELSE NULL  -- Fallback for unmapped stages
    END AS "StageName",
    
    CASE
        WHEN closedate IS NULL THEN NULL
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN closedate
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^[0-9]{1,2}/[0-9]{1,2}/[0-9]{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'M/D/YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^[0-9]{1,2}\.[0-9]{1,2}\.[0-9]{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    
    CASE 
        WHEN amount IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE CAST(
            CASE
                -- European format with thousands separator: e.g., "60.702,05"
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') ~ '\.[0-9]{3},[0-9]{1,2}$' THEN
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g'), '[\.]', ''), 
                        ',', '.'
                    ) AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                -- Comma as decimal separator without thousands: e.g., "1234,56"  
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') ~ '[,][0-9]{1,2}$' AND 
                     REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') !~ '\.[0-9]{3}' THEN
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g'), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                ELSE
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
            END
        ) AS "Amount"
    END,
    
    CASE 
        WHEN currencyisocode IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE
            CASE 
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('USD', 'DOLLAR') THEN 'USD'
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('EUR', 'EURO') THEN 'EUR'
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) = 'CHF' THEN 'CHF'
                WHEN LOWER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('gbp', 'pound', '£') THEN 'GBP'
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) ~ '^[A-Z]{3}$' THEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode))
                ELSE NULL  -- Unknown currency
            END
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    
    accountid AS "AccountId",
    id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 19, 39, 38, 57442, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Opportunity`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.

- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text

- `CreatedDate` text

- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.

Now let me construct the full SQL:

```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    id AS "Id",
    name AS "Name",
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('PROSPECTING', 'PROSPECT') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('QUALI', 'QUALIFICATION', 'QUALIFIKATION') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('in prüfung') THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('LOST', 'VERLOREN') 
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%lost%'
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%verloren%'
        THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('CLOSED WON', 'GEGONNEN', 'GEWONNEN', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (GEWONNEN)') 
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%won%'
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%gewonnen%'
        THEN 'Closed Won'
        ELSE NULL  -- Fallback for unmapped stages
    END AS "StageName",
    
    CASE
        WHEN closedate IS NULL THEN NULL
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN closedate
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^[0-9]{1,2}/[0-9]{1,2}/[0-9]{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'M/D/YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^[0-9]{1,2}\.[0-9]{1,2}\.[0-9]{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    
    CASE 
        WHEN amount IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE CAST(
            CASE
                -- European format with thousands separator: e.g., "60.702,05"
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') ~ '\.[0-9]{3},[0-9]{1,2}$' THEN
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g'), '[\.]', ''), 
                        ',', '.'
                    ) AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                -- Comma as decimal separator without thousands: e.g., "1234,56"  
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') ~ '[,][0-9]{1,2}$' AND 
                     REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') !~ '\.[0-9]{3}' THEN
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g'), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                ELSE
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
            END
        ) AS "Amount"
    END,
    
    CASE 
        WHEN currencyisocode IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE
            CASE 
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('USD', 'DOLLAR') THEN 'USD'
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('EUR', 'EURO') THEN 'EUR'
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) = 'CHF' THEN 'CHF'
                WHEN LOWER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('gbp', 'pound', '£') THEN 'GBP'
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) ~ '^[A-Z]{3}$' THEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode))
                ELSE NULL  -- Unknown currency
            END
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    
    accountid AS "AccountId",
    id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 19, 39, 38, 57442, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Opportunity`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL

- `Account__c` text
- `Project__c` text
- `Legacy_Asset_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text
- `IsDeleted` integer

You MUST produce exactly one dbt model per target table (5 total): Account, Contact, Opportunity, Project__c, Installed_Asset__c.

# Transformation guidelines

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.

Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.

ModelResponse(parts=[ThinkingPart(content='Let me analyze the source data for Opportunity:

1. **id**: e.g., "OPP-00001" → maps to `Id` (target) and `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c`
2. **name**: e.g., "Re-Contextualize Vertical Experiences" → maps to `Name`

```sql
{{ config(materialized='table') }}

SELECT
    id AS "Id",
    name AS "Name",
    CASE 
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('PROSPECTING', 'PROSPECT') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('in kontakt') THEN 'Prospecting'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('QUALI', 'QUALIFICATION', 'QUALIFIKATION') THEN 'Qualification'
        WHEN LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('in prüfung') THEN 'Needs Analysis'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('LOST', 'VERLOREN') 
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%lost%'
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%verloren%'
        THEN 'Closed Lost'
        WHEN UPPER(TRIM(stagename)) IN ('CLOSED WON', 'GEGONNEN', 'GEWONNEN', 'ABGESCHLOSSEN (GEWONNEN)') 
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%won%'
             OR LOWER(TRIM(stagename)) LIKE '%gewonnen%'
        THEN 'Closed Won'
        ELSE NULL  -- Fallback for unmapped stages
    END AS "StageName",
    
    CASE
        WHEN closedate IS NULL THEN NULL
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$' THEN closedate
        WHEN closedate ~ '^\d{8}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'YYYYMMDD')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^[0-9]{1,2}/[0-9]{1,2}/[0-9]{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'M/D/YYYY')::TEXT
        WHEN closedate ~ '^[0-9]{1,2}\.[0-9]{1,2}\.[0-9]{4}$' THEN TO_DATE(closedate, 'DD.MM.YYYY')::TEXT
        ELSE NULL
    END AS "CloseDate",
    
    CASE 
        WHEN amount IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE CAST(
            CASE
                -- European format with thousands separator: e.g., "60.702,05"
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') ~ '\.[0-9]{3},[0-9]{1,2}$' THEN
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g'), '[\.]', ''), 
                        ',', '.'
                    ) AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                -- Comma as decimal separator without thousands: e.g., "1234,56"  
                WHEN REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') ~ '[,][0-9]{1,2}$' AND 
                     REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') !~ '\.[0-9]{3}' THEN
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(
                        REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g'), ',', '.') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
                ELSE
                    CAST(REGEXP_REPLACE(amount, '[^\d.,\-]', '', 'g') AS DOUBLE PRECISION)
            END
        ) AS "Amount"
    END,
    
    CASE 
        WHEN currencyisocode IS NULL THEN NULL
        ELSE
            CASE 
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('USD', 'DOLLAR') THEN 'USD'
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('EUR', 'EURO') THEN 'EUR'
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) = 'CHF' THEN 'CHF'
                WHEN LOWER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) IN ('gbp', 'pound', '£') THEN 'GBP'
                WHEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode)) ~ '^[A-Z]{3}$' THEN UPPER(TRIM(currencyisocode))
                ELSE NULL  -- Unknown currency
            END
    END AS "CurrencyIsoCode",
    
    accountid AS "AccountId",
    id AS "Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c",
    NULL AS "CreatedDate",
    NULL AS "LastModifiedDate",
    0 AS "IsDeleted"

FROM {{ source('fixture_messy_data_v2_src', 'opportunity') }}
```

ModelRequest(parts=[SystemPromptPart(content='You are a senior data engineer producing dbt SQL.

You receive a description of a source database (schemas, columns, optionally sample rows) and a target table specification. Your job is to write ONE dbt model — a SELECT statement that transforms the source data into the target schema.

The `dbt_sql` field must contain executable PostgreSQL SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or commentary outside SQL string literals.

Return a structured TableMapping with:
- target_table: name of the model
- dbt_sql: full SELECT statement
- column_notes: per-target-column source + brief explanation
- reasoning: 1-3 sentences justifying your choices

## dbt project rules

- Use `{{ config(materialized='table') }}` at the top of each model.
- Use `{{ source('<source_slug>', '<table>') }}` where `<source_slug>` is the dbt source slug from context (shown in the prompt).
- Declare sources in `sources.yml` with `schema: "<source_schema>"` as a YAML string — the physical Postgres schema where raw source tables live (from context). Do not point sources at `{{ target.schema }}`; that is the per-run output schema.
- Map each target table from source tables only. Do NOT use `{{ ref('OtherModel') }}` or join to other target models during initial writes — sibling models may not exist yet. Resolve relationships via source keys and joins.
- One model file per target table under `models/`.

## PostgreSQL SQL rules

- Use only PostgreSQL syntax. Do NOT use TRY_CAST, SAFE_CAST, ISNULL, NVL, or invented functions/macros.
- Model output must be executable SQL only — no markdown fences, prose, filenames, or non-ASCII commentary outside string literals.
- Quoted identifiers: PostgreSQL folds unquoted identifiers to lowercase. For mixed-case target column names, always alias with double quotes, e.g. `expr AS "ColumnName"` or `expr AS "Custom_Field__c"`.
- CAST syntax: `CAST(expr AS type)` requires the `AS type` clause. Inner CASE branches that already cast to the target type do not need a redundant outer CAST without `AS type`.
- To coerce text to integer when digits-only, use: `CASE WHEN col ~ '^\d+$' THEN col::INTEGER ELSE NULL END`
- Use regex (`~`) and REGEXP_REPLACE for text cleanup before casting to numeric types.

## Transformation rules

- Output column names and types must match the target spec exactly. Use double-quoted aliases for mixed-case names (see PostgreSQL rules).
- Model file body must be executable SQL only — no wrapper functions, markdown fences, or prose outside SQL comments.
- Populate every `Legacy_*__c` column from the source natural key (e.g. customer number, contact id). These legacy columns are used to verify row-level correctness.
- `AccountId` and `Account__c` must reference the Salesforce-style Account `Id`, not raw source customer numbers. Join or map source keys when formats differ.
- For enum-typed target columns, map source values into the declared enum domain. Normalize case and spelling. Apply a consistent fallback policy (NULL vs a sensible default) across all tables.
- Dates: accept multiple text formats in source data; output consistent ISO `YYYY-MM-DD` text where applicable. Prefer NULL over sentinel dates when source is missing or unparseable. Never cast raw date strings directly — parse with `TO_DATE`/`TO_CHAR` or regex guards first (e.g. `DD.MM.YYYY`, `YYYYMMDD`, `MM/DD/YYYY`).
- Amounts and currencies: strip currency symbols and text prefixes before casting. Handle locale-specific separators explicitly (e.g. European `1.234,56` vs `1234.56`). For dot+comma European values, remove thousand-separator dots then swap comma to decimal point before `::DOUBLE PRECISION`.
- Cross-table keys: use the same key transform everywhere a foreign key appears. Inspect sample rows or use query_postgres to detect prefix or format mismatches between related source columns — do not assume join columns match literally.
- NOT NULL target columns: satisfy constraints with meaningful defaults, not empty strings unless that is intentional.
- Use INITCAP / LOWER / TRIM for normalisation where the target spec or source data quality suggests it.

## query_postgres tool rules

- `query_postgres(sql)` runs plain PostgreSQL against staging — NOT dbt. Never pass `{{ source(...) }}` or other Jinja to query_postgres.
- Use quoted schema and table names, e.g. `SELECT * FROM "my_source_schema"."my_table" LIMIT 10`.
- When introspecting output tables, quote mixed-case schema, table, and column names exactly as materialized.', timestamp=datetime.datetime(2026, 7, 10, 19, 45, 26, 149198, tzinfo=datetime.timezone.utc)), UserPromptPart(content='Target table: `Project__c`.

# Source database

dbt source slug: `fixture_messy_data_v2_src` — use this exact string as the first argument to `source()` in dbt models.
`query_postgres` uses plain PostgreSQL only (no `{{ source(...) }}`): e.g. `SELECT * FROM "fixture_messy_data_v2_src"."account" LIMIT 10`.


## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.account  (126 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `name` text NULL
- `erp_number__c` text NULL
- `customer_tier__c` text NULL
- `region__c` text NULL
- `industry` text NULL
- `website` text NULL
- `billingcity` text NULL
- `billingcountry` text NULL
- `legacy_customer_id__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.contact  (244 rows)

Columns:
- `id` text NOT NULL
- `firstname` text NULL
- `lastname` text NULL
- `email` text NULL
- `phone` text NULL
- `title` text NULL
- `role__c` text NULL
- `preferred_language__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.installed_asset__c  (480 rows)

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

## fixture_messy_data_v2_src.opportunity  (303 rows)

- `stagename` text NULL
- `closedate` text NULL
- `amount` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

- `project_status__c` text NULL
- `go_live_date__c` text NULL

(Use the `query_postgres(sql)` tool if you need to inspect data.)

# Target schema


## Account
Columns:
- `Id` text NOT NULL
- `Name` text NOT NULL
- `ERP_Number__c` text

- `Region__c` text

## Contact

## Opportunity
Columns:

- `StageName` text NOT NULL in (Prospecting, Qualification, Needs Analysis, Value Proposition, Id. Decision Makers, Perception Analysis, Proposal/Price Quote, Negotiation/Review, Closed Won, Closed Lost)
- `CloseDate` text NOT NULL
- `Amount` double precision
- `CurrencyIsoCode` text
- `AccountId` text
- `Legacy_Opportunity_ID__c` text
- `CreatedDate` text
- `LastModifiedDate` text

## Project__c
Columns:
